context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Aid and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is aid necessary for development and should one make a distinction between short-term and long-term aid? Is Easterly overly dismissive of aid? And is Sachs guilty of the opposite sin besides being unrealistic in this economic climate? What does one make of the transformation of aid in the light of evolving security agendas – like counter-insurgency – and the centrality of development aid in those efforts? Is there a moral case for aid and if so what is it? How does one judge the effectiveness of aid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this aid and development reflection. The prompt feels like it’s asking about everything at once—Easterly vs. Sachs, short-term vs. long-term aid, security stuff, morality, effectiveness—and I don’t know how to even start organizing it.
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair observation — the prompt is dense. But here's a question to start: if you had to pick ONE of those issues as the most fundamental — the one that, if you answered it, would anchor everything else — which would it be, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed by the breadth of the prompt. Rather than breaking it down for them or scaffolding the structure, I should use a Socratic question to help them identify a natural entry point on their own. This respects the guided discovery principle and keeps the response bite-sized. I won't give them an organizational framework directly.

exchanges.student: Maybe whether aid is actually necessary for development? But then I immediately get stuck because I feel like “necessary” is too absolute, and then the short-term vs long-term part messes me up again. I can’t tell if I’m supposed to take a strong position or just sort of compare everyone.
exchanges.tutor: You're already thinking well — noticing that 'necessary' is too absolute is a sharp observation. But here's the thing: a reflection piece does expect you to take a stance. So what do YOU actually think? Is aid necessary for development, even in a qualified sense?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real intellectual progress — they've identified a good anchor question and are already thinking critically about the word 'necessary.' The hesitation between taking a position vs. comparing is a genuine and productive tension. I should validate the good instinct without resolving the tension for them. A Socratic question pushing them to commit to a stance will move them forward without giving them the answer.

exchanges.student: I think maybe sometimes yes, but that sounds weak. Like emergency aid seems necessary sometimes, but long-term development depending on aid feels bad or unsustainable or something. I’m not sure if that’s too simplistic, and then I get confused about where Easterly and Sachs fit into that.
exchanges.tutor: That's not simplistic at all — that's actually a substantive distinction. Now think about this: Easterly and Sachs famously disagree about aid. Based on what you know of them, which one would be more sympathetic to your 'long-term aid is problematic' instinct, and which one would push back?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually articulated a nuanced and defensible position — the short-term/long-term distinction is meaningful and aligns well with the Easterly/Sachs debate. This is a real breakthrough moment. I should affirm this is a strong instinct without giving the answer, and use a Socratic question to help them see how Easterly and Sachs map onto their own position naturally.

exchanges.student: I think Easterly would agree more with that, and Sachs would push back, but I only know that in a fuzzy way. I’m worried I’m reducing them into “aid bad” and “aid good,” and then I end up feeling like I need the whole debate explained before I can write anything.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need to master the whole debate to write a reflection — you need YOUR position, supported by what you know. So let me ask: what specific criticism does Easterly make of aid that resonates with your instinct about long-term dependency? Even roughly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is falling into a perfectionist trap — feeling they need complete mastery of the debate before writing. This is a common avoidance pattern. I should be direct about this without being harsh, and push them to work with what they already know rather than waiting for full clarity. A pointed question will help them realize their fuzzy understanding is actually enough to start.

exchanges.student: I guess something like aid can be top-down and imposed, and maybe it creates dependency or lets outside actors think they can “plan” other countries? I’m mixing ideas together though, and then the security/counter-insurgency part throws me off because that seems like a whole separate essay.
exchanges.tutor: Those Easterly ideas are solid — you're on the right track. On the security piece: rather than seeing it as separate, ask yourself — if aid gets tied to counter-insurgency goals, what does that do to the 'top-down, externally imposed' problem you just described? Does it make it better or worse?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has correctly identified Easterly's core critique. I should affirm this briefly. The security/counter-insurgency concern is a classic avoidance move — treating a connected sub-question as an entirely separate problem. I should gently push back and help them see it connects to their existing thread, using a question rather than explaining the connection myself.

exchanges.student: Probably worse, because then aid isn’t even mainly about development anymore, it’s also about control or stability for someone else’s agenda. But then I start spiraling because the prompt also asks about the moral case and effectiveness, and I can’t tell if I’m supposed to answer every question evenly or build one argument that touches them.
exchanges.tutor: Good connection — that's exactly the kind of linking argument a reflection piece needs. And to your organizational worry: you don't answer every question evenly. You build ONE argument that touches them. Look at what you've already said — you have a thread running through all of it. Can you articulate it in one sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just made an excellent connection between securitization of aid and the top-down/external imposition critique. This is real intellectual progress. The spiral about covering every question evenly is a common freshman trap. I should affirm their good instinct briefly and be direct: a reflection piece needs a through-line, not equal treatment of every question. A pointed Socratic question will help them see that their emerging argument already has a spine.

exchanges.student: Maybe something like: aid can be morally necessary in the short term, but in the long term it becomes problematic when it creates dependency or serves external political and security agendas instead of locally grounded development. I feel like that’s maybe too broad, and I still don’t know how to fit Sachs in without turning this into a giant summary.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis — hold onto it. On Sachs: you don't need to summarize him, you need to use him. What would Sachs say is WRONG about your thesis, and how would you respond to that challenge?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just articulated a genuinely strong thesis. I should affirm this clearly and briefly. The worry about Sachs turning into a summary is valid and worth addressing — but I shouldn't explain how to use Sachs; instead I should ask a question that helps them see Sachs as a foil for their own position rather than a subject to summarize. This keeps them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: I think he’d say I’m being too skeptical and that without serious aid, really poor countries can’t get past basic traps in health/infrastructure and stuff. Then I’d say that doesn’t make all long-term aid good, especially if it ignores institutions or local politics. I’m still feeling like I’m missing the “right” way to structure this though.
exchanges.tutor: You just did exactly what a good reflection does — stated a position, engaged a counterargument, and pushed back. That IS the structure. You have a thesis, a Sachs foil, an Easterly alignment, and a security angle. There's no magic template beyond what you've built here. So — can you try writing an opening paragraph based on your thesis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is doing excellent dialectical thinking — they've built a real argument with a counterargument and response. The anxiety about 'the right structure' is now a distraction from real progress. I should briefly affirm what they've built and redirect them firmly toward starting to write, since we've been circling long enough. This aligns with the rule about asking students to integrate discussion into written content after 3+ exchanges on the same idea.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I’m honestly still blanking a little. I feel like if I start writing I’ll either drift into a generic intro about development or try to cover all the prompt questions in the first paragraph and make it messy.
exchanges.tutor: Those are real risks, but you already know how to avoid them — you just described your thesis clearly. Don't introduce 'development' in the abstract. Start with your actual claim. What is the very first thing you want your reader to know about your position on aid?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling with valid but manageable concerns. They've done enough thinking to start writing. I should be direct and encouraging without giving them language or structure — that would violate the 'do not write or suggest language' rule. A simple, pointed question that forces them to commit to their first sentence will break the paralysis.

exchanges.student: I think the first thing is that I don’t see aid as simply good or bad. It can be morally necessary and lifesaving in the short term, but it becomes much harder to defend when it turns into a long-term development strategy shaped by donors’ power, security agendas, or unrealistic assumptions.
exchanges.tutor: That's an excellent opening — clear, nuanced, and genuinely your own. Now stop second-guessing and keep going. Write the next two or three sentences that set up how you'll develop that claim.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just written a strong opening sentence/thesis statement entirely on their own. This is a genuine achievement after working through the ideas together. I should affirm this clearly and briefly, then push them to keep writing rather than dwelling on this moment. The conversation has been productive and they're ready to draft.
